title: Trade Tools for Accelerating the Electric Vehicle Transition: Best Practices from Existing Frameworks
gist: CSIS distills six trade-policy instruments — tariffs, rules of origin, procurement, technology transfer, regulatory standards, and investment — into a two-track framework for India to use trade agreements as explicit EV industrial policy, liberalizing inputs from diversifying partners (Track A: US, EU, Japan) while filtering and screening partners embedded in concentrated supply chains (Track B).

themes.theme: India's EV supply-chain exposure and the two-track framework

insights.claim: India's national EV policy targets 30 percent of all new cars and 80 percent of two- and three-wheelers electric by 2030, backed by FAME I & II, PM-E DRIVE and Production Linked Incentives for batteries, plus over 30 subnational EV policies — but India remains heavily reliant on imported battery cells, critical minerals and advanced components, and its trade policy is an underused lever for closing that gap.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Frames trade-agreement design, not just domestic subsidy policy, as the next major lever for India's EV manufacturing buildout — relevant to component suppliers and investors assessing India as a production base.
insights.locator: India is moving toward electrification of its transportation

insights.claim: The paper proposes classifying India's trade partners into two tracks: Track A "Economic Security Countries" (the US, EU, Japan) that are actively diversifying and restructuring industrial/trade policy to build robust friend-shored supply chains, versus Track B "Economic Hedging Countries" that are expanding EV supply chain prominence by deepening reliance on concentrated geographies, posing potential disruption and geopolitical risk to India.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: This two-track lens is the organizing framework the paper applies to every trade instrument — useful shorthand for predicting how India will negotiate with any given partner.

insights.claim: India's recent free trade agreements with Australia, EFTA, the UAE and the UK account for only a small share of India's actual EV-related imports, while China, Taiwan, Japan and South Korea dominate actual supply — meaning India's trade-agreement diversification on paper has not yet matched its real import concentration risk.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: A concrete gap between India's FTA headline diversification and its actual supply-chain exposure — the real concentration risk with China and Taiwan remains largely unaddressed by current agreements.
insights.locator: Trade data analysis shows that these four partners

insights.claim: India's Chile CEPA negotiations (launched 2025) mark its first explicitly minerals-focused trade agreement; the proposed Japan CEPA upgrade is anchored by a $70 billion investment target; and Chile and Peru together supply over 43 percent of India's extraction-stage critical mineral imports, making both central to India's near-term sourcing diversification strategy.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Identifies Chile, Peru and Japan as the specific near-term deals to watch for India's critical-minerals diversification progress, with concrete negotiation milestones.
insights.locator: The Chile Comprehensive Economic Partnership Agreement

insights.claim: The US imposes a 100 percent tariff on Chinese EVs while the EU layers company-specific countervailing duties atop its standard 10 percent import tariff — a blunt-instrument approach versus a more differentiated one — and evidence shows even modest tariffs on EV inputs can significantly raise final EV prices and slow both adoption and domestic manufacturing scale-up.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Contrasts two live defensive-tariff models (US blunt vs. EU differentiated) that India is weighing as templates for its own China-exposure hedge — relevant to how aggressively India might eventually tariff Chinese EV inputs.
insights.locator: At the same time, experience in transatlantic and U.S.–China–EU trade

insights.claim: The recommended Track A tariff approach is to eliminate duties at entry into force on upstream EV inputs (minerals, electrodes, battery cells), as in the UK-India agreement, to lower domestic manufacturing costs, while the Track B approach keeps MFN or near-MFN rates on battery cells and packs and makes any liberalization contingent on verifiable supply-chain origin to block third-country routing.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Gives a testable prediction for how India's tariff schedules will differ by counterparty in future EV-related trade deals.
insights.locator: Track A: India should maintain open, low-tariff regimes

themes.theme: Rules of origin as an investment magnet and transshipment filter

insights.claim: USMCA's 75 percent regional value content requirement, high steel/aluminum sourcing thresholds and labor value content rules are cited as the most robust example of using rules of origin as active industrial strategy — channeling EV and battery investment (gigafactories, component plants) into North America rather than merely gatekeeping tariff eligibility.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: A template India could replicate to pull EV/battery gigafactory investment domestically via origin-rule design, not just subsidies.
insights.locator: The U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA) provides one of the most robust examples

insights.claim: Cumulation-rule design has concrete, immediate trade consequences: the UK-EU Trade and Cooperation Agreement avoided a sudden EV tariff shock by extending the deadline for tightening local-content rules from 2024 to 2027, while the withdrawal of bilateral cumulation in UK-Canada automotive trade immediately exposed exporters to new tariffs.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Demonstrates that origin-rule cumulation terms are not technical fine print but can trigger or avert real tariff shocks — a specific negotiating lever India should prioritize getting right.
insights.locator: A similar logic underpins the UK-EU Trade and Cooperation Agreement

insights.claim: For Track B partners and reexport hubs in Southeast Asia and the Middle East, the recommendation is to apply the UAE-India CEPA's full anti-transshipment tool kit — HS six-digit tariff-shift rules and 40 percent value-addition thresholds — specifically to battery cells, packs and electrodes, reinforced with supply chain disclosure requirements.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: A concrete anti-circumvention mechanism to watch for in India's upcoming Southeast Asia/Middle East deals, aimed at preventing Chinese content from being laundered through reexport hubs.
insights.locator: Southeast Asia and the Middle East

themes.theme: Procurement and technology transfer as demand and capability levers

insights.claim: India is not a signatory to the WTO Government Procurement Agreement, so any EV-related procurement preferences — such as embedding environmental criteria (life-cycle emissions, charging compatibility) into PM-eBus Sewa tenders — must be secured through bilateral treaty commitments rather than multilateral rules.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: A structural legal constraint: India's procurement-based EV demand tools depend entirely on what it negotiates bilaterally, not on any existing multilateral baseline.
insights.locator: It is worth noting that India is not a signatory

insights.claim: The US-Japan Critical Minerals Agreement lets Japanese-sourced minerals qualify under US EV tax credit rules, incentivizing allied investment in extraction and refining outside concentrated geographies — cited as a template "functional trade agreement," narrowly scoped to a single industrial objective rather than broad market liberalization, that India could replicate with its own mineral-source partners.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Identifies a specific deal structure (narrow, objective-scoped minerals agreement tied to tax-credit eligibility) India could pursue with Chile, Peru or Australia to diversify mineral sourcing.
insights.locator: The U.S.-Japan Critical Minerals Agreement (CMA) is emblematic

themes.theme: Regulatory alignment and investment protection by track

insights.claim: The EU-India FTA, finalized January 2026, already provides zero-duty access on minerals and engineering goods; the report recommends India use the pre-entry-into-force window to negotiate an added battery-standards chapter aligned with the EU Batteries Regulation, building on the UK-India CETA's regulatory cooperation precedent, to cut recertification costs for Indian firms.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: A specific, time-bound negotiating opportunity (the EU-India FTA's pre-EIF window) that market participants tracking India-EU EV trade should watch for.
insights.locator: The European Union and the United Kingdom

insights.claim: Track A regulatory cooperation should pursue mutual recognition and joint standard-setting (e.g., building on UK-India CETA precedent and EU-US mutual recognition talks on EV safety/charging) to reduce compliance duplication, while Track B partners should keep a WTO TBT-anchored, sovereignty-preserving approach that retains India's right to refuse equivalence and avoid long-term technological lock-in.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Shows regulatory policy, like tariffs and origin rules, is being deliberately bifurcated by geopolitical alignment rather than applied uniformly.
insights.locator: Track A: Regulatory cooperation should move toward targeted alignment

insights.claim: A future US-India trade agreement should combine the UK-India CETA's tariff liberalization model for upstream EV inputs, the Australia ECTA's coequal rules of origin, and India-Japan CEPA-style investment protections specifically to attract US capital into Indian gigafactory projects linked to PLI-scheme eligibility.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: A specific blueprint for what a future US-India EV trade chapter should contain — relevant to US investors evaluating India as a gigafactory location under a prospective bilateral deal.

insights.claim: For South Korea, which leads in battery manufacturing but remains embedded in concentrated cathode supply chains, the report recommends pairing UK-India CETA-style origin rules and Japan-CEPA-style investment protections with UAE-CEPA-style tariff-shift and disclosure rules to trace Korean midstream processing routes, and structuring procurement to reward Korean FDI that builds Indian domestic cell capacity rather than treating India as an export destination.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: A partner-specific playbook distinguishing South Korea as a hybrid case — treated with Track A tools for investment but Track B-style transshipment scrutiny given its cathode supply-chain concentration.
insights.locator: South Korea leads in battery manufacturing

themes.theme: Trade agreements as de facto industrial policy

insights.claim: The report argues that trade agreements are already functioning as de facto industrial policy for the EV transition elsewhere — citing the New Zealand-UK FTA, USMCA, UK-EU TCA, Singapore-Australia Green Economy Agreement, and US-Japan CMA — and that India's opportunity is to explicitly design its own agreements around EV supply-chain objectives rather than treat EV provisions as peripheral to broader market access goals.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The overarching takeaway: EV supply-chain strategy should be read directly out of trade-negotiation priorities, not just industrial subsidy announcements, when assessing India's EV manufacturing trajectory.
insights.locator: The EV supply chain should be prioritized as the organizing priority
